title: Space Tech Briefing — July 18, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space enthusiasts! It's Friday, July 18, 2026, and the cosmos is absolutely buzzing with activity! From live calls with astronauts to crucial experiments for deep-space exploration, and a look back at ongoing challenges, let's dive into your essential space technology briefing.

First up, get ready for a truly out-of-this-world experience! France's Cité de l'Espace in Toulouse is set to host a live, real-time call with French ESA astronaut Sophie Adenot. She's currently aboard the International Space Station, and you can experience that direct link-up live from Toulouse. Imagine connecting with an astronaut in orbit – a truly inspiring moment for all!

And speaking of Sophie, she's been incredibly busy on the ISS! On June 12th and again on July 10th, she conducted the fascinating French "Food Processor" experiment. This isn't just a simple kitchen appliance for space; it's a vital step towards preparing for long-duration deep-space missions. This innovative experiment is all about developing food autonomy for astronauts, paving the way for future explorers to sustain themselves far from Earth. Delicious and critical science in one!

Now, let's take a moment to recap a significant development we've been following closely. A recent report from NASA's Office of Inspector General, released last month on June 30th, highlighted the persistent difficulties facing Boeing's Starliner spacecraft. This report also points to the risks for NASA in relying solely on SpaceX for human spaceflight missions to the International Space Station. There's still considerable uncertainty about when Starliner will fly again, and when it will finally be certified for those crucial ISS crew rotation missions. It's a reminder of the complex challenges in developing new human-rated spacecraft.

That's all for your morning space briefing! Thank you for tuning in. Keep looking up, keep exploring, and we'll catch you next time for more out-of-this-world news!
